headlineSummary: Anthropic drew strongly negative sentiment with repeated privacy, trust, and product-quality criticism [E2][E6][E10], while Google Gemini and OpenAI were both only mildly negative and in a close tie on limited evidence [E1][E5].

ranking.summary: Anthropic was the clear negative focus, with comments criticizing data retention, safety overreach, deception, slow desktop performance, and broader trust and strategy concerns [E2][E3][E4][E6][E9][E10][E11][E12][E13][E14].
Google Gemini had a mildly negative note on limited evidence, with one comment criticizing the search-integrated version as the "worst version of Gemini" in a comparison [E1].
OpenAI also showed a mildly negative note on limited evidence, with one comment warning that Codex can mean sending an entire codebase to the provider, creating privacy and competitor risk [E5].

evidence.excerpt: Gemini is not illegal. Pretending the worst version of Gemini is Search is illegal, because it breaks the rules established for Search.

annotations.rationale: Directly names Gemini and characterizes the search-integrated version as the "worst version of Gemini," a clear criticism.

evidence.excerpt: It is actually worse than that. It is at least 30 days. There is an "almost" that is doing a ton of heavy lifting here "deletion after 30 days in almost all cases". My read of that is they can hang onto data for as long as they want

annotations.rationale: In the story context about Anthropic retention, the comment strongly criticizes the policy as effectively allowing indefinite data retention.

evidence.excerpt: Fortunately I can't use Fable anyway, since their hyperactive content flaggers do not let you work on anything remotely biological or medical related (i.e. parse a CSV with some medical content, nope, you're probably a bioterrorist) and you get downgraded to Opus immediately.

annotations.rationale: The comment complains that Fable/Opus safety systems over-trigger and block benign work, expressing strong dissatisfaction with Anthropic's guardrails.

evidence.excerpt: Pretty incredible just how much good will Anthropic managed to burn.

annotations.rationale: Explicitly says Anthropic has burned a large amount of goodwill, a strong negative trust judgment.

evidence.excerpt: A startup that uses agentic coding tools such as Claude Code or Codex is packaging up their entire codebase and sending it directly to their LM provider. Depending on their product, they might be sending it directly to a potential competitor.

annotations.rationale: Claude Code is explicitly named, and the comment warns that using it means sending an entire codebase to the model provider, implying privacy and competitive risk.
Codex is explicitly named in the same warning about transmitting a full codebase to the provider, implying privacy and competitive risk.

evidence.excerpt: The strangest part is that it won't just reject ML research, which I can understand, it will sabotage it silently by using a worse model without revealing it is doing so. It's just an insane level of deception and trust destruction for a company that at most is like 1 year ahead of its competition.

annotations.rationale: In the Fable story context, the commenter accuses the company of silent sabotage, deception, and trust destruction.

evidence.excerpt: I wear a few hats, but as a chemist and I'm not happy with fable. As a statistician I'm not happy with fable. As a data scientist I am not happy with fable. As an academic and a researcher I am not happy with fable. It's useless.

annotations.rationale: The commenter repeatedly states dissatisfaction with Fable and concludes "It's useless," a strong negative quality assessment.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Desktop spawns 1.8 GB Hyper-V VM on every launch, even for chat-only use

annotations.rationale: The title itself frames Claude Desktop negatively by highlighting heavyweight VM behavior even for simple chat use.

evidence.excerpt: I just found a really pointed example of Anthropics lack of craft / rush to build.

annotations.rationale: Directly states Anthropic shows a "lack of craft / rush to build," a strong negative product-competence judgment.

evidence.excerpt: I’ve stopped using Claude on the desktop, just because of how slow the app is to start up and interact with. It’s an absolute clunker;

annotations.rationale: The commenter says they stopped using Claude Desktop because it is slow and calls it "an absolute clunker," a strong negative usability assessment.

evidence.excerpt: This is odd behaviour, and provides some evidence that Anthropic isn't being managed by serious people. With this policy across AWS/GH/Zed/etc, they're taking their massive lead in enterprise/govt sales and handing it to any competitor who can serve a model anywhere near these capabilities with a modestly nice UI.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly says Anthropic may not be managed by serious people and criticizes the policy as strategically self-destructive.

evidence.excerpt: Ugh. I'm sure we're not the only company that's going to face the difficult decision to either stay with Opus 4.8, switch to a different model provider or update and significantly weaken our terms of service around no model re-training, not sending data to third parties and the like.

annotations.rationale: The commenter presents Anthropic's change as forcing an unwelcome choice and weakening privacy-related terms, a clear negative reaction.

evidence.excerpt: That's it. If you have confidential data that you're running with Fable, you're giving that away for free. Maybe you have always been, but now they explicitly ask for it.

annotations.rationale: In context of the Anthropic data-sharing policy, the commenter strongly warns that using Fable means giving away confidential data.

evidence.excerpt: I do place trust in specific people who work at Anthropic, but I wouldn't place trust in Anthropic the organization.

annotations.rationale: The commenter expresses trust in some Anthropic employees but explicitly distrusts Anthropic as an organization, making the sentiment mixed overall.
